Fresh jets for Delta's fleet.

Delta's 767 replacement decision signals the airline industry's shift toward next-generation widebody efficiency and potentially influences Boeing's competitive positioning against Airbus.

Mission to Recover Downed F-15E Aircrew Puts Combat Search And Rescue in the Spotlight

Modern CSAR operations require coordinated deployment of specialized units across multiple domains, demonstrating the critical infrastructure investment needed beyond aircraft procurement.

Effective SRM requires organizations to document why selected controls adequately mitigate identified risks before accepting any residual risk exposure.

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran’s RQ-170 Clone Destroyed in U.S. Strikes, CENTCOM Video Shows

A video shared by CENTCOM shows the destruction of an Iranian clone of the RQ-170 Sentinel, which was reverse-engineered after crashing in Iran 15 years ago. An Iranian clone of the secretive U.S. RQ-170 Sentinel ISR drone was apparently destroyed in an airstrike, an Apr. 2, 2026 video by the U.S....

Reverse-engineered adversary ISR platforms remain operationally vulnerable despite technological parity, highlighting persistent targeting advantages for advanced militaries.

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from investigating crashes to preventing them through systematic hazard identification and mitigation protocols.

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Illuminated in Orion

NASA astronaut Christina Koch reads on a tablet in the dimly lit Orion crew capsule in this April 3, 2026, photo. To the right of the image’s center, CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen is seen in profile peering out of one of Orion’s windows. Lights are turned off to avoid glare on...

Orion's interior lighting design prioritizes window visibility for crew observation, demonstrating critical human factors engineering in deep-space capsule development.

See NASA →

5 Reasons Why Pilots Prefer The Boeing 787 Dreamliner

The aircraft offers exceptional long-haul capabilities.

The 787's flight deck design and fuel efficiency reduce crew fatigue on ultra-long routes, directly improving safety and operational economics for carriers.

Commercial Apr 4, 2026

Why The Airbus A220 Is Becoming More Attractive To Southwest Airlines

A closer look at what the aircraft could mean for Southwest, and why its 737-only operations are now at risk.

Southwest's exclusive 737 fleet strategy faces economic pressure as the A220's fuel efficiency and lower operating costs make fleet diversification increasingly unavoidable.

General Aviation Apr 4, 2026

How Much Does Pilot Training Cost In The US?

The cost of pilot training extends beyond tuition fees, living expenses, simulator time, and other charges that can catch aspiring pilots off guard.

Hidden costs beyond tuition—including housing, simulator fees, and checkride expenses—can inflate total pilot training expenses by 30-50% beyond published figures.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via...

Runway exit design issues at major hubs like LAX create predictable incursion patterns that require procedural modifications beyond pilot awareness training.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Why The C-5M Super Galaxy's 28 Wheels Must Rotate 90 Degrees Just To Retract

The brilliant engineering solution enables a massive payload and efficient flight performance.

The C-5M's 90-degree wheel rotation mechanism solves the geometric impossibility of retracting 28 wheels into a fuselage, demonstrating how extreme payload requirements drive unconventional engineering solutions.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 4, 2026

Iran Attained A Fleet Of F-14s Even Though The US Regretted It – Now They’re Destroyed

During the Cold War, Iran was viewed as a crucial stop-block for Soviet southern expansion, the F-14 played a role in this effort.

Iran's F-14 destruction eliminates a critical capability that sustained regional air superiority for decades despite U.S. export regrets, reshaping Middle Eastern military balance.

Safety Apr 4, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strike fatalities persist because pilots systematically underestimate their inability to react or maneuver away once wires are visually detected at operational speeds.

Aerospace Apr 4, 2026

Artemis 2 in good shape cruising towards the moon

A day after lighting its engine to head to the moon, the Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft is performing well with only minor issues.

Artemis 2's successful trans-lunar injection with minimal anomalies validates critical spacecraft systems for crewed lunar missions resuming in 2026.

Regulatory Apr 4, 2026

Trump budget seeks $372 million cut to US Essential Air Service program

US President Donald Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget proposal takes aim at the Essential Air Service program, calling for…...

Eliminating EAS funding could force rural airports to lose commercial service entirely, threatening connectivity for communities lacking viable ground transportation alternatives.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA Sets Coverage for Northrop Grumman’s CRS-24 Resupply Launch

NASA, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX are targeting no earlier than 8:49 a.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 8, for the next launch delivering science investigations, supplies, and equipment to the International Space Station. Filled with approximately 11,000 pounds of cargo, the Northrop Grumman Cygnus XL...

Northrop Grumman's larger Cygnus XL variant increases ISS resupply payload capacity to 11,000 pounds, expanding research and operational capabilities for the orbiting laboratory.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US A-10 hit during Iran rescue mission, pilot ejects and is rescued

A US Air Force A-10 was hit during combat operations tied to the war with Iran on April…...

Combat damage to A-10s during extended operations highlights the vulnerability of legacy platforms in modern threat environments, raising sustainment and force readiness questions.

See AeroTime Hub →

Industry Apr 4, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across airlines, airports, and flight systems offers immediate efficiency gains without requiring full fleet replacement, making sustainability economically viable during aircraft transition periods.

See MDPI →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

Michael Graham Named NTSB Vice Chairman

The certified transport pilot and NTSB board member will co-head the agency for a three-year term.

Graham's aviation operations background positions the NTSB to strengthen technical accident investigation expertise during a period of evolving aviation risks.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

US and Europe revise GPS interference guidelines as spoofing risks grow

Aviation authorities are warning that instances of GPS interference have spread across a widening range of high-risk airspace,…...

Expanding GPS spoofing incidents across multiple regions signal that aviation infrastructure resilience now requires regulatory harmonization between US and European authorities.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Updated: A-10 Thunderbolt II Crashed Near the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Officials Tell NYT

Almost the same time as an F-15E Strike Eagle went down over Iran, the U.S. Air Force lost an A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft near the Strait of Hormuz. U.S. officials told NYT that the jet’s pilot was safely rescued. While search and rescue efforts for at least one of the crew members of an […]...

The simultaneous loss of two separate U.S. Air Force jets in the Persian Gulf region suggests escalating operational tempo or sustained regional tensions requiring immediate force projection.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Meet NASA’s New Artemis II Science Officers

NASA’s first Artemis II science officers—Kelsey Young, Trevor Graff, and Angela Garcia—are pioneering a new era of mission operations by integrating advanced lunar science directly into Mission Control. Their work will shape how future Artemis crews observe, analyze, and deepen our understanding of...

NASA's new science officer roles embed specialized lunar researchers directly in Mission Control, enabling real-time scientific decision-making during crewed lunar operations rather than post-mission analysis.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Italy’s Argotec plans to scale Florida satellite facility to meet rising US demand

Italy’s Argotec has officially opened its first U.S. satellite production facility, cementing a foothold near Kennedy Space Center in Florida to join other foreign space firms pursuing growing demand from American programs.

Foreign satellite manufacturers are establishing US production hubs to capitalize on government contracts and reduce supply chain vulnerabilities for national security programs.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 3, 2026

US F-15E Shot Down Over Iran, One Crew Member Rescued

The fate of the aircraft’s second crew member is unknown.

The loss of a U.S. fighter aircraft over adversarial airspace highlights critical gaps in combat search-and-rescue coordination and crew survivability protocols in contested environments.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA report proposes sweeping rewrite of Part 141 flight training rules

A 471-page industry report released through the FAA’s Part 141 modernization docket proposes a sweeping rewrite of how…...

The FAA's comprehensive Part 141 modernization proposal signals a major shift toward performance-based standards that could reshape flight training curricula, instructor requirements, and school operations across the industry.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom

Today marks the 100th anniversary of the birth of Virgil I. “Gus” Grissom, born April 3, 1926, in Mitchell, Indiana. As one of NASA’s first seven astronauts, he became America’s second astronaut to fly in space when he launched aboard the Liberty Bell 7 spacecraft on July 21, 1961, just weeks after...

Gus Grissom's pioneering contributions to early spaceflight established critical protocols for crewed spacecraft design and emergency procedures that remain foundational to modern aerospace operations.

See NASA →

Military Apr 3, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 3, 2026

A C-130J awaits its next assignment on the flightline.

The C-130J's continued operational presence underscores the enduring relevance of tactical airlift platforms in modern military logistics and rapid-response missions.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

ZeroAvia edges closer to cert as FAA issues electric engine conditions

ZeroAvia has this week celebrated a “major step” towards certification of its ZA600 hydrogen-electric powertrain, after the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) published special conditions to certify the system’s electric engine component. The FAA published 33 special conditions for...

FAA's 33 special conditions provide the regulatory framework needed for hydrogen-electric propulsion certification, accelerating ZeroAvia's path to the market for regional aircraft retrofits.

See Runway Girl Network →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

White House again proposes steep NASA budget cuts

For the second consecutive year, the White House is proposing a major budget cut for NASA that would significantly impact the agency’s science programs and the International Space Station.

Sustained NASA budget reductions threaten ISS operations and space infrastructure that supports critical aerospace industry partnerships and commercial space development.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Test Pilots and the Road Back to the Moon

For NASA’s first crewed lunar mission since Apollo, aviation experience is one of the clearest threads linking the astronauts aboard Orion.

NASA's Artemis astronauts share core aviation backgrounds, suggesting that traditional pilot expertise remains critical for modern lunar exploration despite advances in automation and spaceflight training.
